perturbation.original_text: Co-Host shall be the exclusive reseller of software products ("Software") at the Originating Locations. The preceding sentence shall not prohibit NAI from (i) reselling Software including NAI Goods (hereinafter defined) from the McAfee Mall; (ii) referencing and linking to sites of strategic partners (other than competitors of Co-Host listed on Part 2 of Exhibit "C") which may also be involved in the resale of Software from such sites; provided that no Software may be purchased on the page of such site which is linked to any Originating Location and further provided that the references and links to the sites of strategic partners shall not be placed on the Online Service Page; and (iii) advertising Software with banners, buttons and other forms of online advertising; provided that any link from such advertising takes the end user to the publisher of the Software and not a reseller of Goods (other than Co-Host). For example, an advertising banner or button for the Windows 98 software program may link back to the website of Microsoft Corporation but not the website of Dell Computer Corporation which is reselling the program.
perturbation.changed_text: Co-Host shall be the exclusive reseller of software products (“Software”) at the Originating Locations. Notwithstanding this exclusivity, NAI reserves the right to also directly resell software products at the Originating Locations, but only during promotional periods. The preceding sentence shall not prohibit NAI from (i) reselling Software including NAI Goods (hereinafter defined) from the McAfee Mall; (ii) referencing and linking to sites of strategic partners (other than competitors of Co-Host listed on Part 2 of Exhibit "C") which may also be involved in the resale of Software from such sites; provided that no Software may be purchased on the page of such site which is linked to any Originating Location and further provided that the references and links to the sites of strategic partners shall not be placed on the Online Service Page; and (iii) advertising Software with banners, buttons and other forms of online advertising; provided that any link from such advertising takes the end user to the publisher of the Software and not a reseller of Goods (other than Co-Host). For example, an advertising banner or button for the Windows 98 software program may link back to the website of Microsoft Corporation but not the website of Dell Computer Corporation which is reselling the program.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'Notwithstanding this exclusivity, NAI reserves the right to also directly resell software products at the Originating Locations, but only during promotional periods' introduces a direct contradiction to the initial statement granting exclusive reseller rights to Co-Host. While Co-Host is intended to be the exclusive reseller of software products, it goes to contradict itself by giving the possibility to NAI of reselling software products at the same locations in promotional periods. This undermines the definition of exclusivity and creates ambiguity about the actual scope of Co-Host's rights.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Co-Host shall be the exclusive reseller of software products (“Software”) at the Originating Locations. Notwithstanding this exclusivity, NAI reserves the right to also directly resell software products at the Originating Locations, but only during promotional periods. The preceding sentence shall not prohibit NAI from (i) reselling Software including NAI Goods (hereinafter defined) from the McAfee Mall; (ii) referencing and linking to sites of strategic partners (other than competitors of Co-Host listed on Part 2 of Exhibit "C") which may also be involved in the resale of Software from such sites; provided that no Software may be purchased on the page of such site which is linked to any Originating Location and further provided that the references and links to the sites of strategic partners shall not be placed on the Online Service Page; and (iii) advertising Software with banners, buttons and other forms of online advertising; provided that any link from such advertising takes the end user to the publisher of the Software and not a reseller of Goods (other than Co-Host). For example, an advertising banner or button for the Windows 98 software program may link back to the website of Microsoft Corporation but not the website of Dell Computer Corporation which is reselling the program. Without limitation on the foregoing, NAI may co-host a comparative shopping service on the Originating Locations. NAI will obtain a written covenant that the comparative shopping co-host will present sellers of Software in a neutral manner and upon request of Co-Host will require removal or alteration of presentations by such co-host on such co-hosted facility which Co-Host reasonably deems to be non-neutral; provided, however, that, notwithstanding the foregoing, Co-Host may be the featured or most prominent Software reseller on any such service.
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the initial statement about Co-Host's exclusivity. The added phrase 'Notwithstanding this exclusivity, NAI reserves the right to also directly resell software products at the Originating Locations, but only during promotional periods' creates a clear and direct conflict with the term 'exclusive reseller'. The contradicted text includes the changed text and subsequent conditions. By giving NAI the ability to resell during promotional periods, the change severely undermines Co-Host's exclusivity and introduces ambiguity about their actual rights, creating a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: NAI reserves the right to change the URL of the Originating Locations from time to time and agrees to give Co-Host as much notice of any such change as is practicable.
perturbation.changed_text: NAI reserves the right to change the URL of the Originating Locations from time to time and agrees to give Co-Host notice of any such change. The type of notice will be at NAI's descretion.
perturbation.explanation: The change replaces "as much notice of any such change as is practicable" with "notice of any such change. The type of notice will be at NAI's discretion". This makes the amount and type of notice completely arbitrary, giving NAI full control and potentially undermining Co-Host's ability to adapt to changes in a timely manner. This contradicts the overall intention of providing reasonable marketing consideration to Co-Host, as mentioned earlier in the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_text: During the Term (as defined in Section 6 (a) below), NAI shall provide the following marketing considerations to Co-Host: (a) Co-Hosting Rights. Co-Host shall be permitted to maintain on the Online Service Page (as defined in Part 2 of Exhibit "A") of the Originating Locations in the manner set forth on Exhibit "B" hereto (and on such other positions as are set forth on Exhibit "B" or as the parties may mutually agree upon in writing from time to time) a hot link to Internet locations specified by the Co-Host (the "Destination") from which Goods (other than Competitor's Goods (hereinafter defined)) may be sold. The web pages at the Destination shall be maintained in accordance with the requirements of this Agreement, including without limitation, Section 2 hereof. "Competitor's Goods" as used herein shall mean the Goods of any of the persons or entities described on Part 1 of Exhibit "C" attached hereto and made a part hereof. The Destination shall not contain any links to any third party sites for the purchase of Competitor's Goods; provided that the Destination will link to the Co-Host Site (which will sell Competitor's Goods).
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation of NAI to provide 'as much notice of any such change as is practicable' to just 'notice of any such change. The type of notice will be at NAI's discretion'. This contradicts the marketing consideration clause, as Co-Host's ability to maintain a hot link to their Destination could be severely impacted by NAI's arbitrary notice, hindering their ability to provide the agreed-upon marketing considerations. The original text implies a reasonable notice period, while the changed text allows for potentially immediate or insufficient notice, directly conflicting with the intent to provide marketing considerations to Co-Host.

perturbation.original_text: Co-Host will conduct its business in a manner that reflects favorably upon the Products and NAI.
perturbation.changed_text: Co-Host will conduct its business in a manner that reflects favorably upon the Products and NAI, unless Co-Host determines in its sole discretion that a less favorable approach would maximize profits. In such cases, Co-Host is authorized to prioritize profit maximization.
perturbation.explanation: The addition "unless Co-Host determines in its sole discretion that a less favorable approach would maximize profits. In such cases, Co-Host is authorized to prioritize profit maximization" directly contradicts the earlier requirement for Co-Host to conduct business in a manner that reflects favorably upon the Products and NAI. It creates uncertainty about the expected standard of conduct and undermines the objective of maintaining a positive image. This can affect Section 9, where it has an effect on Co-Host's responsibilities.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Co-Host represents and warrants to NAI that Co-Host has all right, title, ownership interest and/or marketing rights necessary to provide the advertising materials to NAI, to perform its obligations hereunder and to operate the Destination. Each party further represents and warrants to the other that it has not entered into any agreements or commitments which are inconsistent with or in conflict with the rights granted or obligations incurred by the representing party in this Agreement. Co-Host further represents and warrants that the advertising materials supplied hereunder do not infringe any Covered Country (hereinafter defined) copyright, trademark, or trade secret right. Covered Country shall mean the United States of America and any member state of the European Economic Union. Co-Host agrees that, if notified promptly in writing and given sole control of the defense and all related settlement negotiations, it will defend NAI, its employees, officers and agents, against any claim based on an allegation that (i) advertising materials supplied hereunder infringes a Covered Country patent, copyright, trademark or state trade secret right, or (ii) Co-Host violated any law, statute or ordinance or any governmental or administrative order, rule or regulation with regard to the advertising materials, the operation of the Destination or the manufacture, possession, distribution, use or sale of the Goods. Co-Host will pay any resulting costs, damages and attorneys' fees finally awarded by a court, or agreed to in settlement by Co-Host, with respect to any such claims. NAI agrees that, if the advertising materials become, or in Co-Host's opinion are likely to become, the subject of an infringement claim, NAI will permit Co-Host, at Co-Host's option and expense, to, among other things, procure the right for NAI to continue marketing and using the advertising materials, or to replace or modify them so that they become non-infringing.
perturbation.justification: The changed text allows the Co-Host to prioritize profit maximization even if it reflects unfavorably on the Products and NAI, directly contradicting the original obligation to conduct business in a manner that reflects favorably upon them. This undermines the representation and warranty in the contradicted text that the Co-Host has not entered into any agreements or commitments inconsistent with the obligations incurred in the agreement, as prioritizing profit maximization could lead to actions inconsistent with the original obligation and therefore creates uncertainty and a potential breach of warranty. It also creates ambiguity which conflicts with Co-Host's other obligations listed in the contradicted text, leading to unclear enforcement.
